coredata.dc:description: N, Si, S co-doped graphene quantum dots (N, Si, S-GQDs) were synthesized from waste toner and glutathione (GSH) via a one-pot hydrothermal method. Combined with o-phenylenediamine (OPD), a rapid, visual, and autocatalytic ratiometric fluorescence sensor of N, Si, S-GQDs/OPD was fabricated for detecting Ag(I) and Fe(III) ions by producing 2,3-diaminophenazine (oxOPD) under pH 8, which emits yellow fluorescence at 560 nm while quenching the blue fluorescence of N, Si, S-GQDs at 440 nm due to an inner filter effect. With the increase of Ag(I)/Fe(III), the blue fluorescence of N, Si, S-GQDs at 440 nm was gradually weakened, along with the enhancement of yellow fluorescence at 560 nm. Hence, this color change from blue to yellow under UV light enables semi-quantitative visual detection. The sensor demonstrates high sensitivity with detection limits of 0.016 µg mL−1 for Ag(I) and 0.010 µg mL−1 for Fe(III), and it successfully detects these ions in lake and tap water without pretreatment. The autocatalytic mechanism involves Ag(I) and Fe(III) reduction to Ag nanoparticles and Fe(II), respectively, which further catalyze the reaction, enhancing selectivity and efficiency. The method is cost-effective, simple, and suitable for on-site environmental monitoring.

head.ce:title: Rapid, visual and autocatalytic quantifying Ag(I) and Fe(Ⅲ) by ratiometric fluorescence sensor of N, Si, S-GQDs/OPD

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Preparation of N, Si, S −GQDs and fabrication rules for rapid, visual and autocatalytic quantifying Ag(I) and Fe(Ⅲ) by ratiometric fluorescence sensor of N,Si,S-GQDs/OPD.

N, Si, S co-doped graphene quantum dots (N, Si, S-GQDs) were synthesized from waste toner and glutathione (GSH) via a one-pot hydrothermal method. Combined with o-phenylenediamine (OPD), a rapid, visual, and autocatalytic ratiometric fluorescence sensor of N, Si, S-GQDs/OPD was fabricated for detecting Ag(I) and Fe(III) ions by producing 2,3-diaminophenazine (oxOPD) under pH 8, which emits yellow fluorescence at 560 nm while quenching the blue fluorescence of N, Si, S-GQDs at 440 nm due to an inner filter effect. With the increase of Ag(I)/Fe(III), the blue fluorescence of N, Si, S-GQDs at 440 nm was gradually weakened, along with the enhancement of yellow fluorescence at 560 nm. Hence, this color change from blue to yellow under UV light enables semi-quantitative visual detection. The sensor demonstrates high sensitivity with detection limits of 0.016 µg mL−1 for Ag(I) and 0.010 µg mL−1 for Fe(III), and it successfully detects these ions in lake and tap water without pretreatment. The autocatalytic mechanism involves Ag(I) and Fe(III) reduction to Ag nanoparticles and Fe(II), respectively, which further catalyze the reaction, enhancing selectivity and efficiency. The method is cost-effective, simple, and suitable for on-site environmental monitoring.

ce:keyword.ce:text: N, Si, S co-doped graphene quantum dots

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The detrimental effects of heavy metal ions on human health are well-documented, and the monitoring of heavy metal ions in aquatic systems has remained a persistent societal concern [1,2]. The potential impact of Ag(I) on aquatic ecosystems and human health cannot be ignored [3]. As an essential trace element, iron plays crucial physiological roles in biological systems. However, excessive intake of Fe(III) can cause nausea, vomiting, acute abdominal pain, diarrhea, fever, and even lead to Parkinson's [1] and Alzheimer's disease [4]. The World Health Organization recommends that content of Ag(I) and Fe(III) in drinking water should not exceed 0.1 and 0.3 μg mL−1, respectively [3,5]. Therefore, monitoring of Ag(I) and Fe(III) in water samples is essential for timely identification and control of heavy metal ion contamination, providing effective safeguards for environmental protection and human health.

Techniques used for detection of heavy metal ions include electrochemical methods [6,7], high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [8], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [9], atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) [10,11], fluorescence sensor and colourimetric analysis [12,13]. Owing to its superior detection capabilities including excellent selectivity, heightened sensitivity, straightforward implementation, economic viability, and visual readout potential [14,15], the fluorescence approach has emerged as a prominent analytical method.

Conventional single-emission fluorescence method is prone to background noise and matrix effects [16,17], while ratiometric fluorescence method [18] has a self-calibration function, which not only effectively eliminates the influence of many analyte-independent factors on measurement results, but also effectively improves signal-to-noise ratio and enhances detection sensitivity, accuracy and stability through measurement of ratiometric fluorescence signals [19,20]. Using malic acid and o-phenylenediamine (OPD) as precursors, Zhang et al. [11] synthesized B/Y-CDs with blue and yellow double emission fluorescence by solvothermal method. In the presence of Ag(I), fluorescence at 453 and 584 nm was quenched and enhanced, respectively, based on dynamic quenching and redox reaction mechanism. Hence, a new method for detection of Ag(I) using B/Y-CDs as a probe was established by “double response” ratio fluorescence sensor.

In recent years, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have attracted people's research enthusiasm due to their advantages such as large surface area, high stability and good biocompatibility [21]. Doped graphene quantum dots have often been used as one of the important fluorescent probes for metal ion detection [22–24]. The structure and photoelectric properties of doped GQDs with heteroatoms can be effectively regulated [22]. They could improve the selectivity and sensitivity for probing metal ion. Li et al. [24] synthesized successfully boron-doped GQDs (o-GQDs) using p-phenylenediamine and boric acid/citric acid as precursors. It is helpful for the chelation between o-GQDs and Al(Ⅲ).

In this study, a green and facile one-step hydrothermal method was employed to successfully synthesize N, Si and S co-doped graphene quantum dots (N, Si, S-GQDs), using waste toner as carbon, silicon source, and glutathione (GSH) as N and S sources. Based on this material, we established a self-catalytic, rapid, and visual ratiometric fluorescence sensor for detecting Ag(I)/Fe(III). By employing triethanolamine (TEOA) and NaCl as masking agents for Fe(III) and Ag(I) respectively, we achieved selective detection of individual Ag(I) and Fe(III) ions. The sensor enables rapid, on-site, and visual semi-quantitative detection of Ag(I) and Fe(III) in lake and tap water, as evidenced by the distinct color transition from blue to yellow under UV light illumination with increasing analyte concentrations.

Waste toner (Youcai Technology Co., Ltd.), and the detailed composition and sources of waste toner were as the same as those in our previous work [25], analytical purity glutathione (GSH)/o-phenylenediamine (OPD) (Aladdin Chemical Co., Ltd.), high purity AgNO3/Fe(NO3)3·9H2O (Sinopharm Group Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.), triethanolamine (TEOA) were used.

Air-drying oven (Shanghai Boxun Industrial Co., Ltd.), miniature reactor (Fuzhou Kreis Test Equipment Co., Ltd.), infrared spectrometer (Nicolet 5700, Nicolet Instruments, USA), fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-4700, Hitachi Group, Japan), ultraviolet spectrophotometer (U-3010, Hitachi Group, Japan), X-ray spectrometer (FEI Tecnai G2 F20, FEI Instruments, USA), fluorescence lifetime spectrometer (FLS 980, Edinburgh Instruments, UK), precision pH meter (PB-10, Sartorius Instrument GMBH, Germany), X-ray powders diffractometer (Zeiss Merlin, Germany), and a zeta potentiometer (NANO ZS3600 Malvern, UK) were employed in this work.

0.1 g of waste toner, 0.25 g of GSH were dissolved in 5 mL H2O, fully mixed for 5 min and placed into 23 mL high-pressure reaction vessel. We set the vessel in air drying oven and keep it at 200 °C for 4 h. After cooling, the resulted mixture was filtrated with 0.45 μm aqueous filter. The yellow filtrate with a concentration of 11 mg mL−1 [25] was collected and stored at 4 °C for future use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of N, Si, S-GQDs/OPD ratio fluorescence sensor
ce:section.ce:para: N, Si, S-GQDs/OPD ratio fluorescence sensor was obtained by adding 240 μL of 10 mM OPD, 35 μL of 11 mg mL−1N, Si, S-GQDs, 625 μL of 40 mM B.R. buffer (pH 8), and 3.1 mL H2O in sequence into a 5 mL eppendorf (EP) tube, shaking and rocking it well at room temperature, and placing it out of light for 1 min.

Lake water and tap water samples were taken from Qingshan Lake and the laboratory of the university, respectively. The appropriate amount of water sample was filtered with 0.45 μm aqueous filter, and filtrate was collected for backup.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Ag(Ⅰ) and Fe(Ⅲ) in water samples by N, Si, S-GQDs/OPD sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Analysis of Ag(Ⅰ). In a series of 5 mL EP tubes, 20 μL of 100 μg mL−1 TEOA for masking Fe(Ⅲ) in probing Ag(Ⅰ), 200 μL of lake water/tap water, and their spiked solutions were added to N, Si, S-GQDs/OPD sensor, respectively. The mixtures were then diluted to 4 mL with H2O and thoroughly mixed for 1 min. Subsequently, F 560/F 440 values were measured under excitation at 370 nm.

Analysis of Fe(Ⅲ). In a series of 5 mL EP tubes, 200 μL of 10 mM NaCl for masking Ag(Ⅰ) in probing Fe(Ⅲ), 200 μL of lake water/tap water, and their spiked solutions were added to N, Si, S-GQDs/OPD sensor, respectively. The mixtures were then diluted to 4 mL with H2O and thoroughly mixed for 1 min. Subsequently, F 560/F 440 values were measured under excitation at 370 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: On-site and visual detection of Ag(Ⅰ) and Fe(Ⅲ) in water samples by N, Si, S-GQDs/OPD sensor
ce:section.ce:para: On-site and visual detection of Ag(I): In a series of 5 mL EP tubes, 20 μL of TEOA (100 μg mL−1) and 200 μL of a series of Ag(I) standard solutions with concentrations of 0, 1, 2, 4, 6, 10, 15, 20, 25, and 35 μg mL−1, as well as spiked solutions of Ag(I) in lake water/tap water (0, 0.9, 5, 30 μg mL−1), were added to N, Si, S-GQDs/OPD sensor, respectively. The mixtures were then diluted to 4 mL with H2O and thoroughly mixed for 1 min. The colors of solutions under UV light at 365 nm were measured and compared.

On-site and visual detection of Fe(III): In a series of 5 mL EP tubes, 200 μL of NaCl (10 mM) and 200 μL of a series of Fe(III) standard solutions with concentrations of 0, 0.5, 2, 5, 7, 9, 12, 15, 20, and 24 μg mL−1, as well as spiked solutions of Fe(III) in lake water/tap water (0, 0.5, 10, 23 μg mL−1), were added to N, Si, S-GQDs/OPD sensor, respectively. The mixtures were then diluted to 4 mL with H2O and thoroughly mixed for 1 min. The colors of solutions under UV light at 365 nm were measured and compared.

In order to confirm structure and properties of N, Si, S-GQDs, it was characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), zeta potential, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and X-ray electron spectroscopy (XPS).

Fig. 1 (a) presents TEM image of N, Si, S-GQDs. It exhibits nearly spherical particles, disperses uniformly in solution, with a relatively uniform particle size averaging 6.72 nm. The lattice spacing is measured at 0.2115 nm, which corresponds to the (011) plane of graphene [26,27], indicating that material possesses graphitic characteristics.

Fig. 1(b) shows XRD pattern of N, Si, S-GQDs. A strong diffraction peak at 2θ = 26.43° is in accordance with a highly disordered graphene-like structure, close to (002) plane of graphite [28,29]. This is also in agreement with previous report [30] on other studies of such nanomaterial, further confirming that N, Si, S-GQDs is graphite-like QDs.
In Fig. 1(c), zeta potential of material is −9.14 mV, indicating that the surface of N, Si, S-GQDs is rich in electrons and has a negative charge.

Fig. 1(d) displays FT-IR infrared spectroscopy characterization of N, Si, S-GQDs. The absorption peaks at 3423, 3219, 2979, 1108, 1010, and 1003 cm−1 correspond to stretching vibration absorption peaks of O–H [31,32], N–H [33], C–H in aromatic rings [34], C–C/C–H (from incompletely carbonized waste carbon powder [35]), C-O-C, and O-Si [32], respectively. However, no distinct −SH characteristic peak is observed near 2500 cm−1 [36], which may be attributed to its content being too low for instrument's detection sensitivity. The peak at 1641 cm−1 corresponds to stretching vibrations of C=O and C=C within conjugated structure [37], providing evidence that N, Si, S-GQDs retain graphene structure [28]. The peaks at 695 cm−1 and 1396 cm−1 are, respectively attributed to stretching vibration peak of secondary amines and C-N [32]. C-N represents aromatic ring skeleton vibration absorption peak [34,38] (carbon dots and graphene oxide quantum dots do not exhibit aromatic ring skeleton vibrations [29,31,32]).

To further determine chemical structure of N, Si, S-GQDs, it was characterized using XPS and the results are shown in Fig. 2 (a). It has five peaks at 284.86, 531.33, 399.89, 102.45, 163.34 eV, which are attributed to graphite C 1 s, O 1 s, N 1 s, Si 2p, S 2p, and its content is 56.95 %, 28.27 %, 12.48 %, 0.93 %, 1.37 %, respectively. C 1 s spectrum (Fig. 2(b)) displays five fitted peaks, attributed to C-O/C=O, C-N/C-S, C–C/C=C, C-Si, and organic-C, with corresponding binding energies of 287.95, 286.0, 284.75, 284.3, and 283.6 eV, respectively [39]. O 1 s spectrum exhibits three fitted peaks at 530.8, 531.4, and 532.5 eV (Fig. 2(c)), which are assigned to C=O, C-OH/C-O-C [40], and O-Si, respectively. In N 1 s spectrum (Fig. 2(d)), two fitted peaks located at 399.7 and 401.2 eV are attributed to N-C (Graphene) [28] and N–H [41], respectively. Si 2p spectrum (Fig. 2(e)) reveals three fitted peaks associated with the groups of Si-O (101.6 eV), organic-Si (101.9 eV), and Si-C (102.4 eV) [42]. The two peaks in S 2p spectrum (Fig. 2(f)) correspond to C-S-C (163.3 eV, S 2p3/2) and (164.45 eV, S 2p1/2) [43], respectively. These findings are consistent with FT-IR results, further indicating that graphite-like carbon material is N, Si, and S-doped graphene quantum dots (N, Si, S-GQDs).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of N, Si, S-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. S1 shows fluorescence spectra of N, Si, S-GQDs (λex = 370 nm, λem = 440 nm), UV–vis absorption spectra, and the colors of N, Si, S-GQDs under daylight and ultraviolet lamps (yellow and blue). GQDs typically exhibit two characteristic UV–visible absorption peaks [44]. UV absorption spectrum of N, Si, S-GQDs (blue line) shows a sharp absorption peak at 298 nm, corresponding to π-π* transition of C=C in the aromatic sp2 structure [45], while the broad absorption peak at 310–330 nm is attributed to n-π* transition of C=O or C − N [46]. Compared to the typical absorption peaks of GQDs, UV–visible absorption bands of N, Si, S-GQDs show a slight shift (10–15 nm), which may be attributed to changes induced by doping of heteroatoms [33].

Furthermore, two luminescence mechanisms of GQDs are widely recognized: intrinsic emission from graphene core and extrinsic emission from surface states [44]. Intrinsic emission corresponds to photoluminescence arising from quantum confinement effects [47], edge effects [48], and electron-hole recombination in sp2 carbon domains [49]. While, extrinsic emission is associated with surface defects. These factors collectively endow N, Si, S-GQDs with fluorescent properties. Using quinine sulfate as a reference [50], the quantum yield of N, Si, S-GQDs was determined to be 14.13 %.

As a fluorescent nanomaterial for detection applications, studying fluorescence stability of N, Si, S-GQDs is of significant importance. The acidity of quantum dots may influence their stability due to protonation and deprotonation capabilities of carboxyl groups. Experiments were conducted to investigate the effect of pH values ranging from 2.0 to 12.0 on the fluorescence intensity of N, Si, S-GQDs. As demonstrated in Fig. S2(a), the fluorescence intensity exhibits remarkable stability across a broad pH range (2.0–12.0).

The effect of ionic concentration on the fluorescence intensity of N, Si, S-GQDs was also investigated under conditions of 0–50 mg mL−1 NaCl. As illustrated in Fig. S2(b), the fluorescence intensity of N, Si, S-GQDs did not exhibit significant changes with increasing NaCl concentration, indicating that N, Si, S-GQDs remain relatively stable under high ionic strength. This stability suggests that N, Si, S-GQDs can effectively function as fluorescent probes in solutions with high ionic concentrations.

The photostability of N, Si, S-GQDs was evaluated by measuring the fluorescence intensity of N, Si, S-GQDs solution stored at 4 °C every 30 days. As shown in Fig. S2(c), the fluorescence intensity of N, Si, S-GQDs remained essentially unchanged over a period of four months. These results demonstrate that N, Si, S-GQDs exhibit excellent stability.

The fluorescence intensities of N, Si, S-GQDs at different temperatures (15–75 ℃) were detected. It can be seen from Fig. S2 (d) that when the temperature varies within the range of 15–45 ℃, the fluorescence intensities of N, Si, S-GQDs basically remain unchanged and slightly decrease at 55–75 ℃. However, the variation range is not large, which proves that N, Si, S-GQDs have better temperature stability.

The photostability of N, Si, S-GQDs was verified by continuously irradiating them under UV light for 120 min and measuring their fluorescence intensity every 20 min. As shown in Fig. S2 (e), the fluorescence intensity of N, Si, S-GQDs remained unchanged after 120 min of continuous UV irradiation, demonstrating their excellent photostability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction mechanism for rapid, visual and autocatalytic quantifying Ag(I) and Fe(Ⅲ) by ratiometric fluorescence sensor of N, Si, S-GQDs/OPD
ce:section.ce:para: As illustrated in Fig. 3 , N, Si, S-GQDs were synthesized through a hydrothermal treatment of waste toner and GSH at 200 °C for 4 h. Under weakly alkaline conditions at pH 8, OPD is rapidly oxidized by Ag(I)/Fe(III) to produce 2,3-diaminophenazine (oxOPD), which emits yellow fluorescence at 560 nm. The fluorescence of N, Si, S-GQDs was significantly quenched through an inner filter effect (IFE) arising from the spectral overlap between oxOPD and N, Si, S-GQDs. As the concentration of Ag(I)/Fe(III) increases, the amount of generated oxOPD also rises, leading to a gradual decrease in fluorescence of N, Si, S-GQDs at 440 nm, while fluorescence of oxOPD itself at 560 nm gradually intensifies. Based on this reciprocal rise and fall, a rapid, visual and autocatalytic ratio fluorescence sensor of N, Si, S-GQDs/OPD for probing Ag(I) and Fe(III) was successfully established. At the same time, under irradiation of 365 nm UV lamp, the color of the sensor gradually changed from blue to yellow, and semi-quantitative visual detection of Ag(I)/Fe(Ⅲ) was realized. The mechanism of the method's construction is as follows: Ag(I) can be reduced to Ag0, which then forms silver nanoparticles (AgNPs). In turn, these AgNPs act as potent catalysts and facilitate the reaction between OPD and Ag(I) [51]. Similarly, Fe(III) is reduced by OPD to produce Fe(II), and the resulted Fe(II) can catalyze the reaction between OPD and Fe(III) [52].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of N, Si, S-GQDs/OPD ratio fluorescence sensor for probing Ag(Ⅰ) and Fe(Ⅲ)
ce:section.ce:para: Factors of pH value, OPD concentration and reaction time on N, Si, S-GQDs/OPD ratio fluorescence sensor for probing Ag(Ⅰ) and Fe(Ⅲ) were optimized.

As in Fig. S3(a), the fluorescence intensity of N, Si, S-GQDs at 440 nm also changes barely in pH 2–12 range. Fig. S3(b) shows effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of N, Si, S-GQDs + OPD + Ag(Ⅰ) at 440 nm (blue line) and 560 nm (red line), and on the fluorescence intensity of OPD + Ag(Ⅰ) at 560 nm (green line), respectively. Similar to N, Si, S-GQDs, the fluorescence intensity of N, Si, S-GQDs + OPD + Ag(Ⅰ) at 440 nm still changes little in pH 2–12 range, while fluorescence intensity at 560 nm increases rapidly in pH 2–8 range, and then reaches maximum at pH 8 and declines at pH 8–12. Similarly, the fluorescence intensity of OPD + Ag(I) at 560 nm also increases rapidly within pH range of 2–7, reaches a plateau at pH 7–8, stabilizes between pH 8–10, and then declines sharply at pH 12. This phenomenon may be attributed to protonation of amino groups of OPD in strongly acidic media, which inhibits oxidation reaction between Ag(I) and OPD [53]. Comparing fluorescence intensity at 560 nm for N, Si, S-GQDs + OPD + Ag(I) and OPD + Ag(I) (red and green lines), it is observed that the difference in ΔF 560 is minimal only at pH 8. Fig. S3(c) illustrates fluorescence curves of both at 560 nm, leading to the selection of pH 8 as the optimal condition. Fig. S3(d) presents the fluorescence spectra of N, Si, S-GQDs, Ag++OPD, and N, Si, S-GQDs + Ag++OPD systems at pH 2, 4, 7, 10, 12 to visually illustrate the pH-dependent fluorescence characteristics.

The concentration of OPD influences the fluorescence intensity of product oxOPD at 560 nm, which in turn directly affects the sensitivity of N, Si, S-GQDs/OPD sensing system for detecting Ag(I) and Fe(III). Therefore, using Ag(I) as a representative, the effect of OPD dosage (0, 80, 100, 200, 400, 600, 650, 800, 1000 μM) on fluorescence intensity ratio (F 560/F 440) was investigated. The results as illustrated in Fig. S4 demonstrate that as concentration increases, F 560/F 440 ratio sharply rises with OPD in the range of 0–200 μM, and then changes little within the range of 200–1000 μM, attains peak at 600 μM. Consequently, optimal dosage of OPD was determined to be 600 μM.

Analytical speed is an important parameter. Therefore, selected Ag(Ⅰ) as the representative, influence of response time on reaction between N, Si, S-GQDs/OPD sensor was investigated. As shown in Fig. S5. The fluorescence intensity ratio (F 560/F 440) of sensor quickly reaches platform within 1 min, indicating that response time between N, Si, S-GQDs/OPD sensor and Ag(Ⅰ) is very rapid, and 1 min was finally selected as response time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of N, Si, S-GQDs/OPD ratio fluorescence sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Under optimal conditions, effects of various metal ions, including Ba(II), Cd(II), Mg(II), K(I), Al(III), Na(I), Ca(II), Cr(III), Zn(II), Mn(II), Fe(II), Co(II), Mn(VII), Cr(VI), Cu(II), Fe(III), and Ag(I), on N, Si, S-GQDs/OPD sensor were investigated separately. The results, as shown in Fig. 4 (a), clearly indicate that only Ag(I) and Fe(III) significantly enhance the F 560/F 440 ratio, while other metal ions have almost no effect. This demonstrates that N, Si, S-GQDs/OPD sensor exhibits excellent selectivity towards Ag(I) and Fe(III).

We took Cu(II) as the representative among Cu(II), H2O2, Cr (VI), and Mn (VII), and studied the reasons why they did not affect the selectivity of N, Si, S-GQDs/OPD system at pH 8. The results are shown in Fig. S6. As seen, the interaction between Cu(II) and N, Si, S-GQDs/OPD sensor was very slow. It required 25 h that the intensity of oxOPD at 560 nm reached its maximum. Under the optimal condition of 1 min for N, Si, S-GQDs/OPD sensor probing Ag(I)/Fe(Ⅲ), there was almost no effect. Different from Ag(I)/Fe(Ⅲ), this might be because there was no autocatalysis for Cu(II), resulting in a very slow reaction speed.

To achieve accurate detection of individual ions Ag(I) and Fe(III), TEOA at 0.5 μg mL−1 and NaCl at 0.5 mM were used to mask Fe(III) and Ag(I), respectively. When detecting Ag(I), introduction of TEOA as masking agent effectively eliminates interference from Fe(III) (Fig. 4(b)). Whereas, for detection of Fe(III), NaCl is employed to mask interference from Ag(I) (Fig. 4(c)). Therefore, N, Si, S-GQDs/OPD is proven to be an effective fluorescent sensor that can be used for accurate detection of Ag(I) and Fe(III) separately.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of Ag(Ⅰ) and Fe(Ⅲ) detected by N, Si, S-GQDs/OPD ratio fluorescence sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The analytical performance of N, Si, S-GQDs/OPD ratiometric fluorescence sensor for probing a series of different concentrations of Ag(Ⅰ) and Fe(Ⅲ) was explored, respectively, under optimal conditions and the results are shown in Fig. 5 . The fluorescence intensity ratio (F 560/F 440) exhibits a good linear relationship with both Ag(I) and Fe(III) (Table S1). The linear ranges for Ag(I) and Fe(III) are 0.1–40 and 0.05–25 μg mL−1, respectively, with detection limits (LOD) of 0.016 and 0.010 μg mL−1. Simultaneously, the color of solution changes from blue to yellow under 365 nm ultraviolet light, indicating that by contrast colors of standard and real sample solutions, N, Si, S-GQDs/OPD ratio fluorescence sensor can be used for on-site, visual, and semi-quantitative detection of Ag(I) and Fe(III).

Table S2 compares analytical performance of this sensor with reported fluorescence methods for detecting Ag(I) and Fe(III). The detection limit for Ag(I) in this method is lower than those in references [54,55], similar to that of Qi [56], and higher than that in reference [3]. For Fe(III), the detection limit is significantly lower than those in Chen. [57,58], slightly lower than those in Yang. [59,60], and higher than that in Wang [61]. N, Si, S-GQDs/OPD ratio fluorescence sensor for probing Ag(I) and Fe(III) not only offers advantages of being economical, simple, and rapid, but also features self-calibration capabilities, which can effectively improve signal-to-noise ratio, and make analytical results more accurate and reliable.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism investigation of N, Si, S-GQDs/OPD ratio fluorescence sensor for probing Ag(I) and Fe(III)
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the mechanism of the ratiometric fluorescence sensing of Ag(Ⅰ) and Fe(Ⅲ) using N, Si, S-GQDs/OPD, using Ag(Ⅰ) as a representative example and examined the UV–Vis absorption spectra of N, Si, S-GQDs, Ag(Ⅰ), OPD, Ag(Ⅰ) + OPD, N, Si, S-GQDs + Ag(Ⅰ), N, Si, S-GQDs + OPD, and N, Si, S-GQDs + Ag(Ⅰ) + OPD. The results are shown in Fig. 6 (a). No changes were observed for N, Si, S-GQDs + Ag(Ⅰ) and N, Si, S-GQDs + OPD. However, both OPD + Ag(Ⅰ) and N, Si, S-GQDs + OPD + Ag(Ⅰ) systems exhibited a new absorption peak at 416 nm, which is attributed to oxidation product oxOPD formed between Ag(Ⅰ) and OPD. This indicates that OPD reacts with both Ag(Ⅰ) and Fe(Ⅲ) to generate the oxidation product oxOPD.

TEM was further employed to characterize reduction products of Ag(Ⅰ) with OPD [62] (Fig. 6(b)). The results revealed the formation of silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) with diameters of approximately 20 nm. These AgNPs exhibit strong catalytic activity, which can accelerate the reaction between OPD and Ag(I) [51].

To investigate the presence of Fe(Ⅱ) as the reduction product between Fe(Ⅲ) and OPD, 1,10-phenanthroline (phen) was introduced as a chelating agent. The UV–vis absorption spectra of phen, phen + OPD, and phen + OPD + Fe(III) were systematically examined (Fig. 6(c)). A characteristic absorption band of the [Fe(phen)3]2+ complex was observed in the 350–600 nm range, with a distinct maximum at 510 nm [63]. Furthermore, Fe(Ⅱ) was found to catalyze the reaction between OPD and Fe(Ⅲ) [52].

To elucidate the interaction mechanism between N, Si, S-GQDs and oxOPD, we systematically investigated the fluorescence excitation/emission spectra of N, Si, S-GQDs and UV–vis absorption/fluorescence spectra of oxOPD ((Fig. 6(d)). Spectral analysis revealed partial overlap between absorption spectrum of oxOPD(λab = 416 nm, λem = 560 nm) and both the excitation (λex = 370 nm) and emission (λem = 440 nm) spectra of N, Si, S-GQDs. This spectral overlap suggests the potential occurrence of either IFE or fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between these species [64].

Fluorescence lifetime is considered one of the effective methods to distinguish between IFE and FRET. Fig. 6(e) displays fluorescence lifetimes of N, Si, S-GQDs (τ0 = 6.20 ns) and N, Si, S-GQDs + oxOPD (τ = 5.91 ns), respectively. The results indicate that there is no significant decay in fluorescence lifetime of N, Si, S-GQDs after interaction with oxOPD, suggesting that fluorescence quenching mechanism of N, Si, S-GQDs by oxOPD is primarily attributed to IFE rather than FRET.

To explore the feasibility and practical application of N, Si, S-GQDs/OPD ratio fluorescence sensor for detection of Ag(I) and Fe(III), we analyzed Ag(I) and Fe(III) in lake water and tap water under optimal conditions to validate the practical feasibility of this method. As shown in Table 1 , the detection results and spike recovery rates for Ag(I) and Fe(III) in lake water and tap water ranged from 95.5 % to 114.5 %, with relative standard deviations (RSD) below 5 % (n = 5). Although Ag(I) was not detected in the lake water and tap water samples, the measured concentrations of Fe(III) were 0.13 μg mL−1 and 0.08 μg mL−1, respectively. These results demonstrate that this ratiometric fluorescence sensor exhibits strong potential for detecting Ag(I) and Fe(III) in aqueous samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual and semi-quantitative detection of Ag(Ⅰ) and Fe(Ⅲ) by N, Si, S-GQDs/OPD ratio fluorescence sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 7 displays the color changes of the sensor under 365 nm UV light irradiation for (a) Ag(Ⅰ) standard solutions (0–35 μg mL−1) and spiked real samples, and (b) Fe(Ⅲ) standard solutions (0–48 μg mL−1) and spiked real sample solutions. The solution colors progressively transition from blue to yellow. By utilizing the differences in color changes, a new method for on-site, visual, and semi-quantitative fluorescence sensing detection of Ag(I) in tap water and Fe(III) in lake water was successfully established. This approach offers significant advantages including simple operation, rapid analysis, low cost, and visual detection, demonstrating excellent potential for widespread application.

N, Si, S-GQDs were synthesized by a one-pot hydrothermal method with green way. A novel self-catalytic, rapid, and visual ratio fluorescence sensing method based on N, Si, S-GQDs and OPD was developed for the detection of Ag(I) and Fe(III). Through the self-catalysis action of Ag(I) and Fe(III), OPD is rapidly oxidized to generate oxOPD. Under UV light irradiation, the color of the sensing system solution changes from blue to yellow, enabling visual semi-quantitative detection. This method offers advantages such as simplicity, accuracy, fast response, good selectivity, high sensitivity, and strong anti-interference capability, making it suitable for analyzing and detecting Ag(I) and Fe(III) in complex matrix samples with excellent application prospects.
Rui Zhou: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Miao Dong: Investigation, Writing – original draft. Yiwei Wu: Supervision.
